headline_rest: the constraint I found, and what changed.

case_studies.title: The Invisible Architecture-Review Queue
case_studies.problem: Leadership believed engineering had a productivity problem — stories carried over sprint after sprint, velocity was unpredictable, release dates slipped. The proposed remedy was more estimation workshops and tighter commitments.
case_studies.diagnosis: Six months of delivery data showed dev and test cycle times were fine. The real signal was a large, highly variable delay between 'ready for development' and 'actually started' — caused by a mandatory architecture-approval step that nobody counted, tracked, or even considered a bottleneck.
case_studies.intervention: Made the architecture-review queue visible with explicit metrics and service-level expectations. Delegated low-risk decisions back to teams and reserved architecture involvement for genuinely high-impact items.
case_studies.outcome: ~40% reduction in end-to-end lead time without increasing engineering capacity. Pre-development wait dropped from ~10–15 business days to ~2–5. Planning became credible. Escalation traffic dropped sharply.
case_studies.lesson: Teams rarely behave irrationally. Systems often do. Predictability didn't come from tighter control — it came from making the system observable.

case_studies.title: Kanban at Scale With Almost No Leverage
case_studies.problem: A large regulated bank needed to improve operational and infrastructure delivery across dozens of teams. Many teams tracked critical work in notebooks, spreadsheets, and individual memory — the system literally could not see its own work.
case_studies.diagnosis: With only two coaches for an entire branch, hands-on transformation everywhere was impossible. Resistance wasn't to process — it was to visibility, because visibility exposes prioritization conflicts, capacity limits, and leadership decisions.
case_studies.intervention: Ran coaching itself as a flow system — time-boxed engagements per team, explicit policies, flow metrics, internal champions, then a lighter-touch handover. Built a distributed improvement network rather than a few optimized islands.
case_studies.outcome: Work moved out of notebooks and into a visible, governable system. Managers shifted from anecdotal status reporting to flow-based operational decisions using measurable arrival rates and completion trends.
case_studies.lesson: The goal isn't less control. It's better control. Success was measured by how many teams no longer needed us.

case_studies.title: Delivery Across an Enterprise You Don't Own
case_studies.problem: Client executives at multinational enterprises had no single credible view of delivery risk across distributed teams, vendors, and time zones. Each team held a different picture of reality. Dependencies were discovered late, as surprises.
case_studies.diagnosis: Fragmented reporting meant no shared reality existed. The gap wasn't capability — it was translation. Engineering, business, delivery, client, and vendor teams were each answering different questions and carrying different risks.
case_studies.intervention: Owned the spaces between teams — surfacing cross-team dependencies early enough to manage rather than escalate. Built a single shared view of delivery risk across product, delivery, and client stakeholders.
case_studies.outcome: Client executives had one credible picture of what would ship and when. Teams shifted from reaction to execution. Dependency-related surprises decreased significantly.
case_studies.lesson: The larger the organization, the less important authority becomes and the more important alignment becomes. The most effective move is usually interpretation before solution.

personal.title: This site
personal.description: An AI-built portfolio — Next.js static export, Tailwind v4, all content as JSON, built with Claude Code.

personal.title: Flow-analytics toolkit
personal.description: Bespoke delivery forecasting — Python and Jupyter pulling Jira/ADO APIs for Monte Carlo and throughput forecasting, independent of vendor tools.
